Question 2 — US Baby Names (1910–2014)

Student 25052977

19 June 2026

The brief

For a toy-design agency: which names have been most popular over time, how persistent that popularity is, and the surges that follow culture (TV characters, singers, songs). The agency wants this to guide which character names to put on toys.

Most popular names by decade

The all-time favourites are long-runners that rise and fade over the decades. Several of them also turn up as series character names (red), but that is mostly because common names get reused for characters, so the popularity came first and the shows followed.

How persistent is popularity?

For each year I take the 25 most popular names per gender and check how similar their ranking still is three years later (Spearman rho).

Persistence is high throughout (rho mostly above 0.8): a year’s top names are usually still near the top three years later. But it has softened: average rho is 0.89 before 1990 versus 0.83 from 1990 on. So the agency’s hunch is right: names churn a little faster now than they used to.

Surges that follow culture

These names at least tripled in a single year. Where a Billboard artist with the same first name was charting in the two years before the spike, I flag it as a likely cause.

Name Gender Year prev_count count growth billboard_artist
Jaime F 1976 897 7836 8.7 NA
Cheryl F 1943 550 2875 5.2 NA
Jenna F 1984 1148 5877 5.1 NA
Mariah F 1991 1086 5190 4.8 Mariah Carey
Ethan M 1989 872 4053 4.6 NA
Selena F 1995 865 3804 4.4 Selena
Shaun M 1977 585 2575 4.4 Shaun Cassidy
Ramona F 1928 520 2229 4.3 NA
Brenda F 1939 646 2736 4.2 NA
Darrin M 1965 784 3254 4.2 NA
Gael M 2012 645 2733 4.2 NA
Jase M 2013 1100 4539 4.1 NA
Brett M 1958 606 2119 3.5 NA
Jeremy M 1969 609 2074 3.4 NA
Lizbeth F 2002 754 2553 3.4 NA

The matches are telling: the Mariah spike (1991) lands right after Mariah Carey’s chart debut, Selena surges in the mid-90s, and similar music-driven jumps show up elsewhere. This is the pattern the agency cares about. A name can ride a hit, but (from the persistence result) it will likely fade just as fast, so a toy tie-in should be timed to the surge rather than treated as a lasting trend.

Notes

Counts are national (summed over states). The bubble uses the 15 highest-count names overall; persistence uses the top 25 per year per gender; the surge cross-reference uses the Billboard charts.rds data. HBO character names are used to flag character overlaps. The brief’s 1974 Katina example is the same kind of TV-driven spike, from a soap-opera character that year.
